records.ety: [AS. cōl; akin to D. koel, G. kühl, OHG. chouli, Dan. kölig, Sw. kylig, also to AS. calan to be cold, Icel. kala. See Cold, and cf. Chill.]

defs: Moderately cold; between warm and cold; lacking in warmth; producing or promoting coolness.
Not ardent, warm, fond, or passionate; not hasty; deliberate; exercising self-control; self-possessed; dispassionate; indifferent; as, a cool lover; a cool debater.
Not retaining heat; light; as, a cool dress.
Manifesting coldness or dislike; chilling; apathetic; as, a cool manner.
Quietly impudent; negligent of propriety in matters of minor importance, either ignorantly or willfully; presuming and selfish; audacious; as, cool behavior.
quotes: Its cool stare of familiarity was intolerable.

defs: Applied facetiously, in a vague sense, to a sum of money, commonly as if to give emphasis to the largeness of the amount.
quotes: He had lost a cool hundred.
Leaving a cool thousand to Mr. Matthew Pocket.

defs: A moderate state of cold; coolness; -- said of the temperature of the air between hot and cold; as, the cool of the day; the cool of the morning or evening.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Cooled (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Cooling.]

defs: To make cool or cold; to reduce the temperature of; as, ice cools water.
quotes: Send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water, and cool my tongue.

defs: To moderate the heat or excitement of; to allay, as passion of any kind; to calm; to moderate.
quotes: We have reason to cool our raging motions, our carnal stings, our unbitted lusts.

defs: To become less hot; to lose heat.
quotes: I saw a smith stand with his hammer, thus, the whilst his iron did on the anvil cool.

defs: To lose the heat of excitement or passion; to become more moderate.
quotes: I will not give myself liberty to think, lest I should cool.
